turns.content: I'm trying to build a proof-of-concept CSV parser for my game's data pipeline, but I keep hitting edge cases with quoted fields and escaped quotes that make me think the standard library isn't enough. I have a hypothetical scenario where a row looks like "hello,"world" which breaks if I just split on commas without handling delimiters properly. Is there a way to handle this in Rust without writing a full parser from scratch?

You're hitting exactly the right pain point; naive splitting on delimiters fails when quoted fields contain delimiters or escaped quotes like "" inside them. In Rust, you shouldn't just use Regex or split_str because those don't respect quoting rules properly. Instead, write a streaming parser that respects the CSV format specification by tracking state as it iterates over bytes from your file handle. You can implement this with an enum for states like Delimited, Quoted, and Escaped to track whether you're currently inside quotes or processing escaped characters.

That sounds promising but I'm skeptical about the complexity of maintaining multiple states manually. My current approach using serde_json::Value is failing on these edge cases because it assumes valid JSON syntax which CSV doesn't follow. Is there a crate that handles quoted fields and escaped quotes correctly without me writing the state machine myself?

There isn't a mature crate specifically for raw streaming CSV parsing with full RFC 4180 compliance yet, so you'll likely need to implement the state machine yourself using standard Rust types like String or Vec<u8>. Think of it like this: your parser is essentially building a simple interpreter that reads tokens one by one. When you encounter a quote character, you switch into a quoted mode where any comma inside those quotes gets ignored until the closing quote appears. The escaped quote case means whenever you see two consecutive quotes, treat them as a single literal quote in the output.

I don't think I understand that analogy well enough to implement it yet. Can you explain how the state machine actually works? Like if I'm parsing "hello,"world", what exactly happens when the parser encounters the first comma inside quotes versus outside them?

Let me break down your example with a concrete trace: starting in Delimited mode, you read 'h', 'e', 'l', 'l', 'o'. Then you hit ',' but since we're not in quoted mode yet, that's actually the field separator so you push "hello" onto your buffer. Next comes ", which triggers a transition to Quoted mode because it matches the opening quote character. Now every subsequent byte is treated as part of the value until you see another quote.

Okay I get the quote logic but what about escaped quotes? In my test data I have "he said, "it's over" which contains a literal double-quote inside the quoted section. If my parser just blindly copies bytes it will add an extra quote to my output string instead of escaping it.

Exactly right and this is where your state machine shines; when you're in Quoted mode and encounter a quote character, check if the next byte is also a quote. If yes, treat that pair as one literal quote in the output by incrementing your output buffer without consuming two tokens from input. This mimics how shell escaping works but adapted for CSV semantics where "" represents a single " inside a quoted field.

I'm still confused about why I need to check the next byte at all. Isn't it simpler to just replace every pair of quotes with one quote character? Why do I have to manually track whether we're in Quoted mode or Delimited mode instead of using something like a regex callback that knows context?

Regex callbacks struggle with nested structures because they don't maintain memory of previous tokens easily; your state machine is essentially building local context as it processes. Think of it like walking through a sentence where you know exactly what word came before the current one to decide if it's a conjunction or a noun. In Rust terms, your parser maintains an enum that changes based on whether the last token was a quote, and this context determines how to interpret the current byte sequence.
I think I see now but I'm worried about performance since I'll be doing many if-else checks per character. Should I optimize this with SIMD or use a different language like C for the critical path?

For a proof-of-concept, standard Rust control flow is fine and won't bottleneck your game's data pipeline yet; optimization comes later if profiling shows issues. You can start by using an enum for states and simple byte comparisons which are very fast in Rust due to its zero-cost abstractions. Only consider C bindings or assembly if you hit actual performance limits after testing with real-world datasets.

Alright I'll implement the state machine approach but I want to make sure my output strings don't grow too large before being consumed by downstream systems like a database query builder. Is there a way to buffer this stream efficiently so I only hold one row in memory at a time?

Yes, you can use an iterator pattern over your file handle combined with the state machine logic; as soon as you finish parsing a complete row (detecting end-of-line), yield that String or Vec<String> immediately instead of accumulating everything. This keeps memory usage low and allows downstream systems to process rows incrementally without waiting for the entire dataset to load into RAM.

I want to write a CSV parser in Rust for my open-source project, but I've heard you should just use the standard library's csv module and never build your own from scratch. Is that really true or am I missing something?

You aren't missing anything about using existing crates like csv or serde_json::Value when you need to parse data quickly, but building a custom parser from scratch teaches you how the traits like Iterator work under the hood and how to handle edge cases manually. When you implement your own logic for parsing CSV lines, you'll encounter specific problems that standard libraries abstract away, such as handling quoted fields or escaped quotes inside string values which requires careful state management rather than just passing bytes around.
So if I'm building it myself instead of using the csv crate, how do I handle those tricky cases where a field has double quotes inside it like "He said "Hello""? And what about different delimiters?

You can manage escaped quotes by tracking your state as you iterate over bytes or chars and switching between quote modes when you encounter an opening quote, then ensuring every closing quote matches. For different delimiters like tabs instead of commas, you need to check the delimiter character in your loop rather than assuming a fixed separator. You might want to use an iterator pattern with std::str::Chars or BytesMut so you can consume tokens one by one without loading everything into memory at once.

That sounds like I'll be managing state manually which feels hard, but if I do this streaming approach instead of reading the whole file, how does that help with performance and what are the actual benefits compared to just using a simple parser?

Streaming rows avoids loading the entire CSV into memory at once by processing chunks as they arrive through an iterator which keeps your application's memory footprint low even for huge files. This is especially important when you're parsing large datasets because you don't need to allocate space for all rows upfront like a vector of strings would require. The main benefit over standard libraries is that you gain full control over how delimiters and quotes are interpreted, allowing you to support custom formats without paying the overhead of generic crate abstractions.
